data.title: Starbucks: What The 3-Step DuPont Model Reveals About Valuation
data.description:
Introduction & Key Takeaway

We previously published an article on Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) in May 2024. At that time, we used Greenblatt’s Magic Formula* lens to evaluate the stock and issued a buy recommendation. A core tenet of the Magic Formula framework is to purchase the stock if the business is earning high returns on tangible assets and also valued at a reasonable earnings yield as measured by the operating income relative to the enterprise value. Another principle of the methodology is to redeploy the capital if there is sufficient appreciation and/or if the earnings yield no longer offers a sufficient margin of safety. Please see Tables 1 and 2.

Table 1: Return on Tangible Assets ($ in millions)
2023 2025
Adjusted Operating Income 5779.5 2936.6
Tangible Assets 8162.95 9990.75
Adjusted Operating Income Return on Tangible Assets 70.80% 29.39%
Table 2: Earnings Yield (EBIT/EV)
May-24 Current SBUX 5Y Avg
EBIT/EV (FWD) 5.27% 3.00% 3.91%

Source: The Little Book That Still Beats the Market by Joel Greenblatt. Copyright 2010 and published by John Wiley & Sons, Inc., Starbucks 10-K for 2023 and 2025, Seeking Alpha

There has been a marked deterioration in the return on tangible assets and a corresponding increase in valuation (resulting in a lower earnings yield), which is what prompted us to revisit our thesis. The current earnings yield is lower than the 5-year average and significantly lower than the time the previous article was written.

In this article, we review the operating performance of Starbucks using a different framework. While we do rely on and use the Magic Formula framework in our investing activities, we also like to use different frameworks from time to time because they illuminate different aspects of the company and the valuation process. Using different frameworks also has the benefit of stress testing our investment thesis. When two or more frameworks point to the same conclusion, our confidence is augmented.

In this study, we use a 3-Step DuPont Framework to support our sell recommendation. The 3-step DuPont framework deconstructs a company’s return on equity into three components: asset efficiency, profitability, and leverage. In all three components there has been notable deterioration in Starbucks’ performance. While we acknowledge that the Back to Starbucks plan has shown some progress in the recent quarter, we do not believe the limited dataset in support of the plan is enough to justify the current valuation.

Starbucks fiscal year ends on the Sunday closest to September 30th; therefore, fiscal year 2025 ended on September 28th, fiscal year 2024 ended on September 29th, etc.

Recent Developments and Back to Starbucks

For the fiscal year 2025, revenues from the North America segment accounted for 74.0% of total net revenues, revenues from the International segment accounted for 21.0% of total net revenues, and the Channel Development segment accounted for 5.0% of total net revenues. Channel development includes the sale of Starbucks branded products outside Starbucks stores. Frequently, the products are sold under agreement with PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) and Nestle S.A. (NSRGY, NSRGF).

For the North American segment, net revenues from company-operated stores made up 90.6% of total revenues, and 9.4% were from licensed stores. In the international segment, the split was 76.1% to 22.7% in favor of company-operated stores.

Of the 11,161 company-operated stores at the end of fiscal 2025 in North America, 10,158, or 91.0%, are located in the U.S., underscoring the importance of economic conditions in the U.S. for Starbucks’ business. Of the 9,857 stores in the International segment, 7,594, or 77.0%, are located in China, which highlights the importance of China in Starbucks’ growth plans.

Source: Starbucks 10-K for 2025

With regard to China, Starbucks recently announced a transaction with Boyu Capital that will shift most of the coffee houses in China from the company-operated side of the ledger to the licensed store side of the ledger. Under the joint venture, Starbucks will retain 40% ownership and will continue to own and license the Starbucks brand and intellectual property to the new entity. Boyu Capital’s acquired interest is based on a “cash-free, debt-free, mutually agreed-upon total enterprise value of approximately $4 billion,” subject to adjustments. The international segment generated almost $8 billion in total net revenues with operating margins of about 13% in 2025. Post-transaction, the International segment revenues are expected to fall to about $5 billion because of the equity method of accounting, which is consistent with the new minority position Starbucks will have of the joint venture. The deal is expected to be accretive to consolidated operating margins by about 40 basis points. The transaction is reflective of an asset-light approach adopted for their international business and expansion plans, which is in alignment with the Back to Starbucks plan. The transaction is expected to close in the spring.

Source: Starbucks Investor Day January 29, 2026, and 10-Q for Q1 2026, Earnings Call for Q1 2026

In Q4 2024, Starbucks announced a Back to Starbucks strategy, which, through various initiatives, is intended to bring customers back to the stores and return to growth by revitalizing coffeehouses, enhancing the customer experience, and improving efficiency. Based on the progress they are seeing, the CEO has indicated that they expect 3% comparable sales growth globally and in the U.S. for the next three years. By fiscal 2028, an important milestone for the strategy, Starbucks expects to deliver 5% consolidated net revenue growth and 13.5% to 15% consolidated operating margin and an EPS of $3.35 to $4.00. Taking their best-case scenario and today’s intraday share price of $96.15, that comes to a fiscal 2028 P/E ratio of about 24x.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Asset Turnover

The asset turnover ratio measures how efficient each unit of asset is at generating revenue. Please see Exhibit 1 for Starbucks' asset turnover ratio over time.

The horizontal dashed line is the median value for the period, and we prefer the median value instead of the average value because it dampens the impact of outliers. The asset turnover ratio in 2025 is slightly below the median value for the period and is also lower than 2024, even though total revenues for 2025 grew 2.8% versus 2024. This is because average total assets in the denominator grew at a much faster 4.2%.

Please see Exhibit 2 for quarterly data. We annualized the quarterly revenue figure to arrive at the asset turnover ratio in this exhibit.

The ratio shows clear improvement for Q1 2026 versus Q1 2025 and is above the median value, which supports the view that there has been progress made in the Back to Starbucks strategy. However, the ratio is still well below the Q1 2024 figure.

Starbucks does not separate and report assets attributable to the North America segment, but they do report revenues and long-lived assets for the U.S. annually. Let us zoom in and look at the ratio for the United States. Please see Exhibit 3.

The ratio is still below the median. Here is an excerpt from their recent 10-K filing explaining the importance of the North America segment, of which the U.S. is the main contributor to operating performance.

“Given its maturity and role as the primary source of operating cash flow, reduced performance in North America could limit our ability to fund international expansion, strategic initiatives, and shareholder returns”

Profitability

Please take a look at Exhibit 4 for Starbucks’ margin trends from 2016 to 2025.

Let us take a closer look at comparable recent quarter margins. Please see Exhibit 5.

Starbucks turnaround plan is not yet taking hold in obvious areas of the business. Let us again zoom in to the North American segment for Q1 information.

Next we turn to a measure of leverage.

Leverage

Please see Exhibit 7.

Starbucks leverage ratio has increased. Our interpretation of the leverage ratio is long-term debt plus equity market capitalization divided by equity market capitalization.

S&P has a BBB+ issuer rating with a negative outlook outstanding. Moody’s has a Baa1 issuer rating and has remarked that Starbucks in on watch for a possible downgrade. Fitch withdrew their ratings of Starbucks in 2022 for commercial reasons.

Return on Equity (ROE)

For equity holders, the return on equity is an important measure of how much value the business can create. With the DuPont framework, the return on equity can be estimated as the product of the total asset turnover, the net profit margin, and the leverage ratio which we have covered. Please see Exhibit 8 for the return on equity derived from these three ratios.

It is early days for the Back to Starbucks plan to gauge the effectiveness of the plan. Having said that, there is clearly more work to be done. The ROE is well below the median value of 15.2%, and, in the recent Investor Day, Starbucks indicated eventually reaching a payout ratio of 50%. This would suggest a sustainable growth rate of 7.6%.

What valuation would you give a business that has a sustainable growth rate of 7.6%?

Let us take a closer look at the trailing P/E ratio of Starbucks over time. Please see Exhibit 9.

Starbucks currently trades at a forward P/E* of 41.56x and a trailing P/E of 47.93x against a median value of 29.65x from the exhibit.

Source: Seeking Alpha. The P/E multiples used from Seeking Alpha are Non-GAAP

In Exhibit 10 we show the P/E ratio and the ROE for Starbucks.

Risks

Given that we have a sell recommendation, risks to our thesis imply that the operating performance of Starbucks improves and that the market further rewards any improvement in operating performance with a higher valuation. While we are skeptical of the second part of the statement, we do acknowledge that improvements in Starbucks operating performance is plausible. Here are some Back to Starbucks initiatives we believe could improve their operating performance.

For Starbucks, over 50% of U.S. revenue is booked by 11 a.m. which means any improvements in throughput could be impactful. Starbucks has already seen improvements in their target of getting their orders fulfilled inside of four minutes and plans to introduce the Mastrena 3 in 2027. The Mastrena 3 is expected to cut in half the current 70 seconds it takes to pull four shots of espresso while doubling the capacity.

Secondly, Starbucks has improved their mobile app and is enhancing their rewards program, which could also be important drivers of incremental business for Starbucks. Beginning in March, Starbucks will introduce three tiers of membership to their rewards program, which is intended to increase frequency and incentivize customers to reach higher membership tiers. If half of active rewards members transact just one additional time per year, Starbucks has said that this would lead to an increase of $150 million to their top line. In Q1 2026, Starbucks reached an all-time high of over 35 million 90-day active members. Also, in Q1, rewards and non-rewards transactions both grew for the first time in nearly four years.

A third area where meaningful upside could buoy the stock is their international expansion. Starbucks expects to grow coffeehouses internationally at double the rate of North America. If they continue to employ an asset-light, licensed store model for their international expansion, operating margins will expand because of the contribution to the margin from the faster-growing, licensed store model.

Final Thoughts

While we acknowledge that the Back to Starbucks plan may gather momentum in the next several quarters, we believe there is just too much optimism baked in the current price and not enough margin of safety. Taking the totality of the data, including our estimate of the sustainable growth rate, we don't think a premium valuation is warranted. Investors should take advantage of the current price and consider selling their shares.

data.title: Coherus: Downgrade To 'Hold' On Potential Advancement Of Anti-CCR8 CHS-114
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Coherus Oncology (CHRS) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Coherus: Biosimilar And Immuno-Oncology Pipeline Setup Could Pay Off". With respect to this article, I noted that the company was gearing up to receive FDA approval of its anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody LOQTORZI [toripalimab] for the treatment of patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma. I'm happy to state that the company did indeed end up receiving approval to target both 1st-line and 2nd-line nasopharyngeal carcinoma patients. Sales of LOQTORZI have been okay, but not as good as they should be to make a difference in boosting the stock price. The company has shifted its focus towards the development of its anti-CCR8 antibody CHS-114 [tagmokitug] for several types of cancers and then the IL-27 antagonist monoclonal antibody casdozokitug for the treatment of patients with 1st-line hepatocellular carcinoma [HCC].

What I want to highlight specifically below would be the development of CHS-114 in combination with LOQTORZI for the treatment of patients with 2nd-line head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC] being explored in a phase 1b study. Initial data from this study targeting this pool of patients is expected in mid-206. The truth is that there are multiple shots on goal for this company during this year. Other readouts expected from the development of CHS-114 would be the ongoing phase 1b study in 1st-line/2nd-line esophageal squamous cell carcinoma [ESCC] patients in mid-2026 and then another phase 1b/2a study of CHS-114 targeting fourth-line colorectal cancer [CRC] patients expected in the 2nd half of 2026.

That was just on the basis of CHS-114, as the IL-27 antagonist candidate casdozokitug is being evaluated in a randomized phase 2 study targeting patients with 1st-line HCC. The point of bringing this candidate up is that data from this mid-stage trial is expected to be released in mid-2026. I had a "Strong Buy" rating because I thought that LOQTORZI would do well in targeting patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma. However, this was not the case, as sales have not gone as well as they should have. With respect to CHS-114, there has been some strong biomarker data that I will go over below in targeting recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients, but it remains to be seen if the data to be released in the 1st half of 2026 lives up to expectations. For these reasons, I believe that it is important to downgrade this stock to a "Hold" rating. It is important that the company shift its biosimilar nature into a full-fledged oncology development company. The hope is that the several sets of data to be released in 2026 accomplish this mission.

CHS-114 Establishes Biomarker Data In Recurrent/Metastatic Head And Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma Patients

The company has a host of data readouts that are expected to happen throughout 2026, but for the purpose of this article, I want to emphasize its program advancement of a phase 1b study evaluating CHS-114 alone as a monotherapy and in combination with LOQTORZI [toripalimab] for the treatment of patients with recurrent/metastatic Head And Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma [HNSCC]. The company released biomarker proof-of-concept data establishing the fact that the anti-CCR8 antibody CHS-114 can deplete target CCR8+ Tregs while sparing non-CCR8+ Tregs. This and much more is its mechanism of action [MOA], which I will be going over below. Before going in depth on the biomarker data released thus far, plus any catalysts to come from this specific program of the targeting of recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients, I believe it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being.

Head And Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma [HNSCC] is characterized as a cancer of the squamous cells [outer layer of skin and mucous membrane] of a patient. This type of cancer can affect multiple areas of the person's body, and they are:

  • Mouth
  • Throat
  • Nose

The thing is that these patients with HNSCC experience a multitude of symptoms, and they are as follows:

  • Abnormal patches or sores forming
  • Unusual bleeding in the mouth
  • Sinus congestion that won't go away
  • Enlarged lymph nodes

The thing is that this order comes about from altered DNA cells, like many other cancer types, but there are risk factors that cause someone to increase their chance of having HNSCC. Some risk factors for increasing the risk of having this cancer are smoking, chewing tobacco, and heavy alcohol consumption. The thing is that LOQTORZI as a PD-1 inhibitor alone is not the entire plan of this company. It wants to add this drug in combination with CHS-114 [tagmokitug] and casdozokitug. The company believes that if it can successfully develop CHS-114 + LOQTORZI for the treatment of patients with 2nd-line HNSCC, then it can go after a $4.4 billion market opportunity [Coherus Oncology Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #7]. The truth is that the targeting of HNSCC is just one indication that the company can go after it with a two-pronged approach of CHS-114 + LOQTORZI. PD-1 inhibitors alone are not effective in many types of cancers, such as [Coherus Oncology Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #13]:

  • Pancreatic cancer
  • Colorectal cancer
  • Liver cancer
  • Ovarian cancer
  • Many others

That is, anti-PD-1 inhibitors are not suitable for approximately 70% of cancer patients. Why is this the case? That's because one problem would be the fact that the cancer itself has low PD-L1 expression. Secondly, there is low infiltration of T-cells in the tumor microenvironment [TME]. If this is the case, then how is CHS-114 to work well with LOQTORZI in conquering this and more? It is simply because of the mode of action for each. CHS-114 is put in place to remodel the TME. This drug, as an anti-CCR8, is able to deplete regulatory T cells [Tregs] from the TME and not from healthy tissue. It also allows for the infiltration of CD8+ T cells to come about. This does both turn cold tumors to hot ones and also involves the remodeling of the TME. Where an anti-PD-1 comes into play here, like LOQTORZI, would be to boost T-cell activity against the tumor itself. Furthermore, it is important to note that the beauty of depleting Tregs is that it can be enhanced by other therapies through partnerships. This could be done as follows [Coherus Oncology Corporate Presentation, January 2026, slide #19] :

  • CHS-114 to be combined with antibody drug conjugates [ADCs] to enable immune activation in cold tumors
  • CHS-114 to be combined with T-cell engagers and CAR-T to enable remodeling of the TME for T-cell activation
  • CHS-114 to be combined with radiation - this is because radiation involves T-reg resistance and this can be overcome with CHS-114 in place

The point is that CHS-114 is the only known selective monoclonal antibody [mAb] to date, as it binds only to CCR8+.

The company is in the process of running an open-label phase 1 dose-escalation and dose-expansion study to evaluate the use of CHS-114 alone and in combination with LOQTORZI [toripalimab] to treat patients with advanced solid tumors. With that being said, though, the company released data from the dose-expansion arm of this study targeting patients with recurrent/metastatic Head And Neck Squamous Cell Carcinoma [HNSCC]. The clinical data itself, presented at the 40th Annual Meeting of the Society for Immunotherapy of Cancer [SITC], showed that the drug did indeed achieve its MOA. This was observed in 10 patients in the dose expansion monotherapy arm and then 6 patients as part of the combination LOQTORZI arm. Both CCR8+ and FOXP3+ T-reg densities were depleted by large margins as follows:

  • Depletion of CCR8+ Treg density by 74%
  • Depletion of FOXP3+ Treg density by 43%

At the same time this was done, CCR8- Tregs in healthy tissue were spared. This shows the selective nature of CHS-114 I just went over above. Remember above, where I state that the drug also remodels the TME? This was also achieved, as evidenced by the fact that these recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients experienced an increase of CD8+ T cell density by 73%. This was only biomarker data; thus, there are still some ways to go in terms of the advancement of this program. However, it at least shows that the intended MOA is indeed being achieved to potentially obtain an improved clinical outcome for these patients.

I believed it was important to go over this program because of the initial data being released from the phase 1b study. But more important than this, it is expected that the company is going to provide a data update from this early-stage trial of CHS-114 in combination with LOQTORZI to treat these recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients in mid-2026. On top of the fact that there are several other catalysts/milestones expected to be released throughout 2026 as well [Coherus Oncology Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #6].

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Coherus Oncology had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $191.7 million as of September 30, 2025. As I noted above, the company is not really generating a substantial amount of revenue with LOQTORZI for the approved indication of nasopharyngeal carcinoma. This is evidenced by the fact that revenue for this drug was $11.2 million in Q3 of 2025, which is only a 12% increase from Q2 of 2025. The truth is that this biotech didn't have a lot of cash on hand, and this is because of what is stated specifically in its 10-Q SEC filing.

It noted that it believed its cash on hand would only be enough to fund its operations for at least 12 months from the date of this SEC filing, which was filed on November 6, 2025. The company did have an ATM offering agreement in place with Cowen and Company [TD Cowen] that it could utilize. This was an ATM in place with approximately $64.9 million worth of common stock available to use.

It didn't use this ATM at all but instead chose to close a public offering of common stock. Such an offering that the company had done helped it to raise about $50.1 million in proceeds before deducting expenses. The company burns roughly $52.2 million of cash per quarter. This is broken down into $27.3 million in R&D expenses and then $24.9 million in SG&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Coherus Oncology. The first risk to consider would be in terms of LOQTORZI net revenue. The company is not making a lot yet on this drug alone for the approved indications. The hope is that it can use this differentiated PD-1 monoclonal antibody with other drugs like CHS-114 to treat patients with several types of solid tumors. The company posted Q3 2025 net revenue of LOQTORZI of $11.2 million, which is only a minor 12% increase from the prior quarter. The risk here is that there is no assurance that Coherus is going to be able to post such another increase in the next quarter to be reported. Nor that sales of this drug will continue to grow at a respective pace with only the approval of targeting patients with nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

The second risk to consider would be the ongoing phase 1b study using CHS-114 in combination with LOQTORZI to treat recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients. As I have shown above, the initial biomarker data was strong, showcasing that the drug was able to achieve its MOA of CCR8+ Treg depletion density of 74% and remodel the TME. It is expected that the company is going to release additional data from this phase 1b study of this combination targeting these patients in mid-2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the next set of data to be released from this study is going to be positive. Nor that the stock market is going to view such clinical data in a favorable manner to boost shareholder value.

The third and final risk to consider would be that of the expansion opportunities. As I mentioned in the beginning, the company is not just only evaluating CHS-114 in combination with LOQTORZI to treat patients with recurrent/metastatic HNSCC. It is also in the process of going after 2nd-line gastric cancer, 1st-line/2nd-line ESCC patients, and 4th-line colorectal cancer patients. Data from several of these programs are expected throughout 2026, and the risk with these is that there is no assurance that one or more of these data readouts are going to end up being positive. Nor that the clinical data produced from them will be good enough to warrant the company to continue to advance them in other clinical trials at a later time.

Conclusion

Coherus Oncology has done well to get to the point it is at with CHS-114. This Treg depleter alone can do well in achieving its MOA. However, since the company has a differentiated anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody in place that it could use, it has decided it is best to combine it with this other candidate. As I went over in this article, CHS-114 is important in handling the depletion of CCR8+ Tregs in tumoral tissue and remodeling the TME thanks to the boost of CD8+ T cells. The purpose of the PD-1 drug is to be put in place to boost T cell activity even further against the tumor. With the need to continue to grow LOQTORZI for the time being [possibly by combining it with CHS-114 and casdozokitug], plus the need to establish additional data in targeting patients with recurrent/metastatic HNSCC in mid-2026, I believe that this stock should be downgraded to a "Hold" rating.

data.title: Figma Has Not Figured Out How To Stand Out
data.description:
Introduction

Figma (FIG) is an interface design and mockup platform used for rapid prototyping and graphics editing. The firm went public in July 2025 but since then has faced a significant and prolonged drawdown, as it is more than 80% below its IPO price, as you can see in the below chart from Seeking Alpha.

FIG is reporting earnings on Feb 19, 2026, and in the below section we will review their upcoming earnings, recent highlights, and overall trends that are impactful. We ultimately believe that FIG simply does not stand out enough, and is overvalued from a financial perspective, so it is a sell.

Earnings Review

FIG reported earnings on Feb 19th, and the shares went up during the after market session and into the normal trading day. Earnings per share came in at 8 cents versus 7 cents expected, and revenue clocked in at $303.8M versus $293.15M expected.

Overall, this represented a 40% growth in revenue on a year-over-year basis for the quarter, which is strong growth—and the firm guided for $315-$317M in revenue for the upcoming quarter. This implies 38% growth, and is higher than the estimate of $292M.

Tech and software players have been getting hit hard recently, and while we remain bearish in the medium term on this company, it is undeniable that their recent earnings were stronger than expected—hence the stock market reaction during the trading day. While this slate of earnings was stronger than expected, we are hoping to see continued investment into their core business and building a differentiated overall product in the future to better stand out.

Interesting partnerships, but the actual outcome is yet to be determined

It was reported earlier that FIG has partnered with Anthropic to bring Claude Code workflows directly into FIG, which will then be able to make the code into frames to edit. This is a useful feature to be sure - there are certainly use cases for many design teams and product managers that could benefit from this. However, it remains to be seen how adoption for this product is in real life. In our view, it is a cool gimmick, but we believe that there is little moat here for FIG to stand out in the long run. With how ambitious players like Anthropic have been in integrating themselves into workflows such as in finance and computer science, what will stop them or another AI model builder in the future from launching a FIG competitor? This remains a major risk for the company, and it does not lead to a stable long-term position for FIG in our book.

SaaS has suffered recently; winners stand out with differentiated product

While FIG is used by a significant number of corporations, the fact that there is so much competition in this sector will ultimately decide which SaaS firm is successful or not against the AI threat that looms. For companies such as Salesforce or Workday, which are integrated into a vast majority of enterprises, this risk of being displaced by AI remains lower. For something like FIG, which is 1) trying to stand out ahead of legacy player Adobe, and 2) trying to stand out amongst upstart peers such as Canva, Framer, and Sketch, this remains a tough multi-front battle. While it certainly is possible that FIG can ultimately win and achieve its vision, they will have to survive a brutal fight for share, which is something that requires time and cash. As their net income remains negative and ending cash flows remain unstable, we are uncertain that FIG has what it takes to adequately compete against the market.

Valuation

In order to come to an estimated valuation for FIG, we employ a peer multiples analysis. For this case we will use EV/Sales [FWD]. We use this metric because it normalizes for different capital structures across the peers. Also, many of the peers (including FIG) have negative net incomes, so we do not use an earnings based metric such as P/E. Finally, we look at the FWD metrics rather than the TTM since FIG has high revenue growth (48.36% YOY), so the metric based on TTM will be off by almost 50% very soon.

For this analysis we look at the following peers: ​​Atlassian Corporation (TEAM), monday.com Ltd. (MNDY), HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS), Asana, Inc. (ASAN), Adobe Inc. (ADBE), Zoom Communications, Inc. (ZM) and Autodesk, Inc. (ADSK).

These peers were chosen either because they operate in a similar industry (enterprise-focused B2B SAAS), have a similar strategy (product-led growth), or have a similar product (design/creative software).

Let’s look at the EV/Sales [FWD] of the group:

Company

EV/Sales [FWD]

FIG

9.94

TEAM

3.41

MNDY

1.7

HUBS

3.18

ASAN

2.01

ADBE

4.15

ZM

3.93

ADSK

6.81

Looking through, we can quickly see that FIG is the highest by a meaningful margin. The next highest EV/Sales [FWD] is ADSK’s, which is still lagging FIG by ~31%.

The average EV/Sales [FWD] for the peers is 3.60 which is lower than FIG’s by 2.76x. FIG's current EV is $10.39B. Based on the peer evaluation, the EV should be 2.76x smaller at $3.76B. To convert to a market cap, we subtract FIG’s debt of $58.5M and add their current cash value of $1.66B to arrive at a market cap of $5.36B. Compared to the current market cap of $11.99B, this represents a 2.24x downside (or 55.30%).

Putting this into per-share terms, our calculated valuation is $11.54 compared to a current price of $25.86.

Risks to thesis

News or rumors of possible acquisitions could rapidly boost shares

As the shares have dropped over 80% since IPO, the company could soon become an acquisition target for larger tech companies that are looking to capture users, technology, or intellectual property. FIG has a market cap of only ~$11.2B today, which means that the cost to acquire the entire firm outright has declined to where it could be a drop in the bucket for a larger SaaS or tech company to buy them.

Figma has a loyal customer base and retreating competition

In their Q3 2025 announcement, the company reported a Net Dollar Retention of 131%, meaning that not only are they keeping their customers, but their customers are increasing their spending over time. One of the biggest competitors to Figma (Adobe XD) has not seen much investment from Adobe. We see that their release page since at least February 2025 has only included bug fixes and other improvements, no real product development. Additionally, there are reports that the product is effectively in maintenance mode.

Both of these factors could lead to higher and higher sales growth for FIG, meaning their valuation metrics could improve over time.

Conclusion

To wrap up, while FIG has certainly been hammered since its IPO, and shares are at all-time lows, we still believe there is room to go down further. It does not have a true differentiator, and while there is no strong direct competition (other than Adobe, XD which is in maintenance mode), there are several potential up-and-coming competitors such as Canva and Framer. Their inconsistent financials and unsteady cash position also do not give strong hopes for long-term battling in this sector.

Given the clearly high valuation (55% overvalued by our calculations) and uncertain continual growth, we rate FIG as a strong sell.

data.title: Portland General Electric: Move To The Sidelines Or Trade Out - Your Choice (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
On June 8, 2025, I penned a Strong Buy recommendation on electric utility Portland General (POR), Portland General Electric: Undervalued And Buy For Income, But It's Not Risk-Free, when it was trading at $40.87. Since then, POR has returned 29.5%, including dividend distributions. At the current valuation and its future prospects, there are other electric utilities offering better potential. I am downgrading my opinion to Hold, at best, or OK to Sell. The basic description of Portland General Electric’s operating business remains the same as June 2025, and I refer to my previous article for that data.

Who is Portland General Electric?

Portland General Electric is a regulated electric utility serving the Willamette Valley, Portland metro, the I-5 industrial corridor from Salem to Vancouver, WA, and short stretches of the Columbia River Gorge both east and west of Portland. POR generates about 87% of its electricity needs through a combination of natural gas (36% of power provided), hydroelectric (26%), wind and solar with battery backup (19%), and coal (6%), with purchased power representing 13%, and is mostly imported from Canada. According to thefly.com, POR imports 525 MW of power annually, worth ~$1 billion.

Population growth and data center opportunities have driven a steady demand for electricity. Portland General Electric services 950,000 customers, and its service territory covers about half the population and two-thirds of the economic activity for the State of Oregon. 62% of electric demand is from commercial and industrial customers. Metro Portland is home to several trans-Pacific subsea internet and communication cables, and the area is ripe for additional data center buildouts. In July 2025, CEO Maria Pope reported on data center and trans-Pacific opportunities:

In addition to Intel's (INTC) recent $36 billion announcement [expansion of its current facility in the ‘Silicon Forest’ high-tech corridor northwest of downtown Portland], Lam Research (LRCX) is completing a new R&D facility in Tualatin. And Analog Devices (ADI) and Siltronic (SSLLF) are expanding in Beaverton and Portland respectively. We're also seeing meaningful growth among our region's data center sector. These customers are enabled by the transpacific subsea fiber landings on the west side of our service territory, similar to the trans-Atlantic network landings in Northern Virginia.

What has changed?

Two important items have changed and altered the investment landscape for investors.

First is the valuation of Portland General Electric stock. Since my June 2025 article and as of 2/18/26, POR has offered investors a 29.5% total return, which dividendchannel.com calculates to an annualized gain of 42.4%. POR stock has moved from $40.87 on June 8 to $51 on Feb 18, and investors have collected three distributions of $0.525 each. In June, POR was trading at a 31% discount to its sector median valuation and a 26% discount to its own 5-yr average. On a TTM basis, SA reports the current price reflects a 10% discount to peers and a 9% premium on its own 5-yr average valuation. Below is a graphic from dividendchannel.com of POR stock performance compared to the sector ETF, Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU):

Since June, Portland General Electric has reversed its discount valuation, and, although it still trades at a slight discount to its peers, it is valued at a premium to its historic valuation. It is fair to postulate that POR is fully valued.

Secondly, Oregon utility oversight is not as attractive as other states, and it seems to be getting worse. As discussed in the June article, Portland General Electric is under the microscope to reduce its Return on Equity from its previously allowed maximum of 9.50% to 9.34%, as of Jan 1, 2025. Oregon regulators had kept the 9.5% allowed ROE since 2020 but last year reduced the number. With inflation still blossoming and with higher financing costs tied to higher interest rates since 2020, the reduction in allowed ROE will negatively affect POR’s profitability and its long-term shareholder returns. Oregon is considered as average for regulatory oversight.

Of greater concern to investors is the latest move by utility regulators to reduce carbon emissions by 90% by 2050. Forbes offered an in-depth article on the Oregon regulatory environment, using natural gas usage as an example, but is very applicable to all utilities.

In the face of that, the Dept of Environmental Quality placed into its Oregon Climate Protection Program rules a requirement that the State work with the Oregon Public Utilities Commission to ensure that natural gas companies do not pass on all the cost of decarbonization to their customers. This means that the State wants utilities to absorb the costs. The likely result of this is to save money, utilities will engage in less maintenance and fewer infrastructure upgrades, neither of which is good for the people of Oregon in the long term. [emphasis added by author]

This more restrictive regulatory adjustment comes at an inopportune time for POR as the state is struggling to find new business investment, especially considering two important corporate moves out of Oregon. Coffee maker Dutch Bros (BROS) is moving from Grants Pass to Arizona, and Tektronix, subsidiary of Ralliant (RAL), the state's largest employer, is moving to North Carolina. While data centers are expanding in Oregon, especially with interest from Asian-bound undersea cable operators, a diverse business base is critical to long-term economic growth. The exodus trend of West Coast companies in general should be negative for utility investors. Although Oregon is still home to Nike (NKE) and Columbia Sportswear (COLM) as stable employers, should either decide to leave the state, it would be a serious blow to the state’s corporate psyche.

Electricity pricing favors other areas of the country

On Dec 29, 2025, the Wall Street Journal published an article titled Be Prepared to keep paying more for electricity. Included were two intriguing graphics, with the balance of the article now behind the paywall. The first is a graphic titled “2024 average electricity price for all customer types, cents a kilowatt-hour” and the second is “Inflation-adjusted change in electricity prices from 2019 to 2024 for all customer types”.

The color-coded map of states is categorized into 4 groups. Of interest to investors should be the states in the two lighter brown groups in the first graphic and the two lighter blues in the second.

https://www.wsj.com/business/energy-oil/be-prepared-to-keep-paying-more-for-electricity-31462755

While Oregon is not in the highest group of average price and inflation-adjusted changes, it becomes obvious which states offer relatively lower electricity rates and relatively lower rates of change. With utility affordability becoming both a political and economic issue, these graphics should resonate with utility investors. Utilities serving light brown and light blue states should offer lower investment risks from affordability issues than utilities in states in the darker shades.

Recent Developments, Earnings

On Feb 17, management announced in a press release it was buying 805 MW of generating assets located in neighboring Washington and regulated utility distribution for 140,000 customers in Walla Walla and Yakima from PacifiCorp (BRK.B) for $1.9 billion. According to the press release, the price equates to 1.4x the estimated 2026 rate base, and as of 2/19, POR had an enterprise value of $10.96 billion. The $1.9 billion addition represents a 17% increase in the post-acquisition enterprise value. Management believes the acquisition will be accretive to earnings in the first year after completion and supports its view of enhancing POR’s earnings and dividend growth profile. The graphic below from the 4th qtr earnings presentation represents the new service territory post-acquisition.

Portland General Electric announced it was issuing 9.5 million shares, raising $480 million for acquisition financing, and increasing share count to 121 million shares for an 8.4% dilution. Interestingly, this acquisition will be made with John Hancock Life Insurance affiliate Manulife Infrastructure Fund III, L.P. The new venture will be majority owned and operated by POR and will be classified as a subsidiary of POR. According to Utility Dive, an industry publication, POR expects Manulife to contribute $600 million. In addition, management is seeking regulatory approval to restructure the company as a holding company, with three operating units: Portland General Electric, transmission assets, and the Washington-state assets.

My opinion is that this purchase does not substantially move the needle and provides little valuation improvement this year. While Oregon reduced its allowed ROE to 9.34%, Washington offers a bit more competitive environment and has an allowed ROE of 9.50%. Washington has a similar regulatory environment to Oregon, and creating a bigger footprint in an average-rated jurisdiction should not get investors overly excited.

In the same press release, management announced full-year 2025 earnings and offered guidance for 2026. Portland General Electric GAAP financials earned $2.77 a share, non-GAAP of $3.05. Management forecasts POR will earn $3.33 to $3.53 a share and believes it will generate 5% to 7% long term earnings growth.

Catalysts/Risks

Wall Street and management are both optimistic about Portland General Electric’s prospects. While the majority of analysts still rate POR as a Hold, their price targets have been moving up. Management has stated it can increase earnings by 5% to 7% annually, which would elevate POR as an attractive utility investment. There is plenty of interest in data centers and subsea cable expansion to increase electricity demand, and POR has forecast a 3% to 5% annual electricity demand growth over the next few years.

If these positive developments materialize and there is a turn in the regulatory environment whereby Oregon utilities are able to fully recoup a fair return on government mandated investments, Portland General Electric could have an attractive future.

Recommendation

Regulated utilities depend solely on state regulations to generate profit, as the state controls utility pricing, among other aspects of utility operations. In my opinion, Oregon is becoming what I have feared for decades – it is becoming un-investable. When the state dictates massive capital expenditures to reduce carbon emissions and forbids these expenses from being included in rate base calculations, the only other funding source is through lower long-term corporate profitability. While this recommendation focuses more on macroeconomic events rather than multiple company-specific prospects, the utility sector is very different than others. Utility regulations vary from state to state, and the differences should not be underestimated.

Investors can either take their profits and invest in utilities in more favorable states, or continue to hold, relying on an attractive dividend yield on cost. POR’s current yield of 3.9% is unexciting, but longer-term holders could have a yield on cost approaching 5%+.

data.title: Japan's Moment: Elections, Flows And Global Opportunities
data.description:
By Jeremy Schwartz, CFA

Investors are increasingly grappling with a familiar problem: U.S. equities are expensive, concentration risk is high and forward returns may look different than the last cycle. That does not mean opportunities are scarce. It means they are shifting.

Japan is one of the clearest examples. The combination of attractive valuations, improving domestic fundamentals and renewed global investor attention is creating an unusually compelling setup. Add evolving political dynamics and a changing global security backdrop, and Japan looks less like a tactical trade and more like a structural allocation investors should reassess.

Why Japan Looks Different This Time

A useful place to start is the relative opportunity set. The S&P 500 is not at bubble levels, but it is priced above its long-term norms. When markets trade at elevated multiples, the math tends to work against investors. Future returns typically compress and the margin for error shrinks.

Japan, by contrast, offers a very different valuation and risk premium profile. Although sovereign bond yields have risen, Japanese equities still offer a significantly higher equity risk premium. That is an important signal that stocks remain more compelling relative to bonds than they are in the U.S.

Higher Japanese yields are not automatically competition for stocks. Given starting valuations, Japan's equity market still looks attractively priced for long-term investors.

The global valuation gap is not marginal. It is significant. We see this in Figure 1 where we note an array of different equity indexes compared to two of WisdomTree's Japanese equity funds. Those two are:

  • The WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ), a strategy designed to track the performance, before fees, of the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Index. This strategy focuses on large market capitalization, dividend-paying exporters of Japan, all the while hedging the currency exposure between the Japanese yen and U.S. dollar.
  • The WisdomTree Japan Opportunities Fund (OPPJ), a strategy designed to track the performance, before fees, of the WisdomTree Japan Opportunities Index. This strategy focuses on certain stocks that Berkshire Hathaway has publicly indicated as strong investments, certain stocks with particularly strong shareholder yield characteristics, and certain thematic characteristics. The currency exposure between the U.S. dollar and Japanese yen can also be dialed up and down based on market conditions.

Figure 1: Valuation Ratios

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, MSCI, S&P as of 1/31/2026. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Our two Japanese equity funds trade at a meaningful discount to U.S. large cap benchmarks, with forward price-to-earnings (P/E) valuations roughly nine turns below the S&P 500. Lower starting valuations historically provide a wider margin of safety and stronger long-term compounding potential.

Rising Yields: A Risk or a Feature?

One of the biggest questions investors raise is whether Japan's shifting interest rate regime is a problem. The more helpful framing is why yields are rising.

Japan's yield curve has steepened in recent quarters. Much of that steepening reflects improving macroeconomic conditions, including stronger growth and modest inflation. Those are the right reasons for rates to rise.

It also matters that Japan's debt dynamics differ from many Western economies. A large share of Japanese government debt is owned domestically, including by the Bank of Japan. Japan is not dependent on foreign demand to fund itself in the same way others are. Higher rates can even support consumption by improving returns for domestic savers and recycling savings back into the economy.

This is a meaningful shift. Japan is moving from economic stagnation to a more normal cycle where growth, inflation and yields interact in healthier ways.

Elections, Policy Direction and the Defense Theme

Political catalysts matter when they accelerate structural change. Japan's latest election cycle and the policy direction that emerged have sharpened investor focus on several themes, including household support measures and national security.

Policy priorities have included potential tax relief and other initiatives aimed at reinforcing a more durable domestic recovery. At the same time, a firmer stance on China and a clearer push toward rearmament signal that defense spending may become a multi-year priority.

Investors have already seen how geopolitics can create sustained spending cycles. Japan's position in Asia and its alignment with U.S. strategic interests suggest that defense investment could become a long duration theme. Investors may gain exposure to a structural spending cycle without needing to assume an escalation scenario.

AI, Demographics and Japan's Productivity Opportunity

Japan's aging population has often been framed as a headwind. In an AI-driven world, that dynamic can shift.

AI and automation can help offset workforce constraints in aging societies by improving productivity and supporting output. Japan sits at the intersection of demographic pressure and industrial strength. It has an ecosystem of companies that participate in automation, advanced manufacturing and semiconductor supply chains.

Japan's relevance to AI infrastructure and enabling technologies adds another structural reason investors are reassessing the market. This is not simply a cyclical rebound story. It is a long-term positioning opportunity tied to global technological change.

Portfolio Construction: Japan as a Diversifier

A common investor objection is that single country exposure equals more risk. In practice, risk depends on how an allocation behaves inside a portfolio.

Correlation data highlights how differently Japanese equities have historically behaved relative to U.S. large cap—notably DXJ and OPPJ had a very low correlation with U.S. equities—showing how they can add diversifying properties to a portfolio.

Figure 2: Performance

Source: WisdomTree Fund Comparison Tool, as of 1/31/26. Past performance is not indicative of future results. You cannot invest directly in an index. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted.

Calendar Year Returns

Japanese equities have also demonstrated competitive or less severe downside characteristics relative to U.S. large caps over the past decade—note the 2022 downside markets when the S&P 500 sold off and Japanese funds performed positively that year—showing that low correlation property.

The worst calendar year for the U.S. markets over the last decade was in 2022 when the S&P 500 declined -18%. Japan's worst year was in 2018 when it also declined 18-19%.

Currency Matters: Why Hedging Can Be a Feature

Currency effects can materially shape long-term outcomes. Japan offers a clear example of how currency exposure can alter investor returns over time.

A currency-hedged approach allows investors to focus on equity fundamentals rather than the yen's path. When interest rate differentials are wide, hedging can also generate positive carry, meaning investors are compensated for neutralizing currency risk.

This is not a forecast about the yen's direction. It is a recognition that currency is a separate risk factor. For many U.S.-based investors, hedging the yen has historically reduced volatility and improved the clarity of the Japan equity exposure.

Strategy and Portfolio Application

Investors looking to express the Japan thesis have several implementation paths within WisdomTree's lineup.

  • The WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ) provides a currency-hedged approach designed to capture Japan's equity opportunity while neutralizing yen exposure.
  • The WisdomTree Japan Opportunities Fund (OPPJ) offers a more opportunity-driven expression of Japan, emphasizing companies with strong fundamentals, shareholder return discipline and dynamic currency management.

From a portfolio construction perspective, Japan can serve as a strategic overweight within the international sleeve. For example, in a portfolio allocating 20 percent to international equities, investors may consider maintaining 10 to 15 percent in broad developed exposure and allocating 5 to 10 percent directly to Japan. This structure preserves diversification while leaning into a market that offers differentiated valuation and correlation characteristics.

Conclusion

Japan's investment case today is not about short-term headlines. It is about a market that combines attractive valuations, improving domestic momentum, structural tailwinds from technology and reform and meaningful diversification benefits.

For investors facing elevated U.S. valuations and a compressed equity risk premium, Japan stands out as a differentiated global opportunity. A disciplined, portfolio-aware approach to implementation can help investors position for what may be a durable shift in global leadership.

Important Risks Related to this Article

DXJ: There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Derivative investments can be volatile and these investments may be less liquid than other securities, and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund, it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. Please read the Fund's prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund's risk profile.

OPPJ: There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Derivative investments can be volatile and these investments may be less liquid than other securities, and more sensitive to the effect of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund, it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Dividends are not guaranteed, and a company currently paying dividends may cease paying dividends at any time. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index. The composition of the Index is governed by an Index Committee and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund's prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund's risk profile.

Jeremy Schwartz, CFA, Global Chief Investment Officer

Jeremy Schwartz has served as our Global Chief Investment Officer since November 2021 and leads WisdomTree’s investment strategy team in the construction of WisdomTree’s equity Indexes, quantitative active strategies and multi-asset Model Portfolios. Jeremy joined WisdomTree in May 2005 as a Senior Analyst, adding Deputy Director of Research to his responsibilities in February 2007. He served as Director of Research from October 2008 to October 2018 and as Global Head of Research from November 2018 to November 2021. Before joining WisdomTree, he was a head research assistant for Professor Jeremy Siegel and, in 2022, became his co-author on the sixth edition of the book Stocks for the Long Run. Jeremy is also co-author of the Financial Analysts Journal paper “What Happened to the Original Stocks in the S&P 500?” He received his B.S. in economics from The Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania and hosts the Wharton Business Radio program Behind the Markets on SiriusXM 132. Jeremy is a member of the CFA Society of Philadelphia.
